text: The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to create a small window in an avascular portion of the mesosalpinx, pulling down to separate the tissue.
The surgeon uses the Vessel Ciliary Extend instrument to apply counter traction by pushing up and laterally, aiding in the manipulation of tissues during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates effective traction and counter traction techniques to manipulate the mesosalpinx, uterine ovarian ligament, and vessels, extending up to the round ligament.

The surgeon creates a small window in the lateral one-third of the round ligament using a hook, allowing for counter traction. The Vessel Ciliary Extend is positioned around the round ligament to facilitate its removal. The hook is then used with cautery to manipulate the medial aspect of the ligament.
The surgeon continues the dissection around the uterus, preparing to complete the separation of the uterine structures.
The surgeon performs a posterior dissection of the broad ligament during a second hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects above the uterosacral ligament, ensuring its preservation during the procedure.
The surgeon performs the same dissection and cautery technique on the opposite side, creating a small window in the lateral one-third of the round ligament and applying counter traction with a hook.
The surgeon creates an avascular window by maneuvering the hook instrument down and medially, while extending the vestal ciliary up and laterally.
The surgeon focuses on the corneal region of the uterus, preparing for further surgical manipulation in this area.
The surgeon identifies and navigates through a more avascular plane, likely between tissue layers, minimizing bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the broad ligament, which appears easier to separate from surrounding tissues in this avascular plane.
The surgeon uses a hook with energy to lift the fallopian tube, maneuvering under the fimbriated end to preserve the uterine ovarian vessels.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers near the uterine ovarian vessel, ensuring precise placement to avoid inadvertently sealing the vessel during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a hook to apply traction, visibly pulling on the tissue to facilitate dissection in the avascular plane of the broad ligament.
The surgeon uses the hook instrument to pull and manipulate tissue, demonstrating its advantage over scissors by allowing precise control similar to using a finger.
The surgeon uses the hook to move laterally and begins to take the tissue, ensuring careful manipulation to preserve the uterine ovarian vessels.
The surgeon uses a hook to apply medial tension on the round ligament, manipulating it downward while preserving the uterine ovarian vessels.
The surgeon uses a hook to apply medial tension and carefully slips a vessel, extending it underneath the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses an instrument to take an additional bite, removing the remaining connective tissue and vessels that are securing the ovary and uterus.
The surgeon completes the posterior dissection, carefully staying above the utero sacral ligament while releasing the ureter to ensure it is out of the dissection plane.
The surgeon makes a frowny face incision to release the utero sacral ligament arch from the back of the colpotomy ring, followed by a smiley face incision to initiate an early colpotomy.
The surgeon performs an early colpotomy incision to facilitate the release of the endopelvic fascia surrounding the uterine vessels.
The surgeon manipulates the uterine vessels to create a plump appearance, facilitating easier sealing and removal during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon backfills the bladder anteriorly, likely using a catheter to introduce fluid, which helps in visualizing and protecting the bladder during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon lifts the peritoneum straight upwards, providing better access and visibility to the underlying structures during the hysterectomy.
The surgeon identifies the bladder in the surgical field, visible as a distinct structure during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon releases the anterior vessels and makes an early incision using monopolar energy, with visible cauterization effects on the tissue.
The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to cut and release the connective tissue around a vessel, targeting the anterior branch of the cervical vessels.
The surgeon completes the same procedure on the opposite side, mirroring the actions performed on the initial side.
The surgeon uses the Vessel Sealer, which exhibits a one to two millimeter collateral spread of energy, to seal vessels during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to efficiently cut and seal tissue, minimizing collateral spread of energy and blood loss.
The surgeon demonstrates a technique using the Vessel Sealer, an instrument designed for efficient cutting and sealing of tissue during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon slides the instrument off the side of the colpotomy ring, maintaining precision in the surgical field.
The surgeon moves the instrument laterally before returning medially, demonstrating precise control over the tissue manipulation during the procedure.
The surgeon uses the Vessel Sealer to create a small pedicle, effectively preventing back bleeding from the vessel.
The surgeon takes an additional bite of tissue using the Vessel Sealer, positioned right off the side of the colpotomy ring.
The surgeon slides the vessel sealer around the vessel, preparing to seal it during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses the vessel sealer to efficiently seal the vessel, preventing any back bleeding.
The surgeon uses a hook to maneuver the vessel into the jaws of the vessel sealer, preparing for efficient sealing.
The surgeon seals the vessel laterally and then medially, creating a small pedicle before cutting.
The surgical field shows no visible oozing or bleeding, indicating effective hemostasis.
The surgeon releases the vessel from the jaws of the vessel serra after securing it with the hook.
The surgeon releases a red line, likely a vessel or tissue structure, using surgical instruments.
The surgeon manipulates the endopelvic fascia surrounding the vessel, positioning the vessel sealer around the vessel for sealing.
The surgeon efficiently seals and cuts the vessel using a vessel sealer, ensuring there is no back bleeding during the process.
The surgeon completes the tissue dissection, ensuring no visible oozing or bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon releases the endopelvic fascia and slides the vessel sealer down the space surrounding the vessel, resembling the motion of inserting pants into a pant leg.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to seal tissue laterally, then moves medially to cut, creating a clear pedicle. The vessel sealer is extended further down the tissue.
The surgeon releases the colpotomy ring during the hysterectomy, reducing tension on the surrounding vessels to improve sealing.

The surgeon uses suture cut needle drivers in the right hand and a long bipolar fenestrated grasper in the left hand. The grasper, which is longer and has more teeth than the Maryland and PK instruments, is utilized for dissection due to its increased strength and torque.
The surgeon takes a five millimeter wide by five millimeter deep bite of the vaginal mucosa, ensuring precise angles during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon closes the vaginal mucosa by taking a five millimeter wide by five millimeter deep bite, using precise suturing techniques.
The surgeon demonstrates a suture technique by taking a five millimeter wide by five millimeter deep bite of the vaginal mucosa, ensuring precise closure.
The surgeon moves to the opposite side of the surgical field, preparing to place a suture at the other angle of the vaginal mucosa closure.
The surgeon incorporates the vaginal mucosa using a five by five bite technique, preparing for a second layer closure.
The surgeon carefully lifts the tissue underneath the previously placed suture, preparing to secure the area with additional suturing.
The surgeon identifies the uterosacral ligament, visually distinguishing it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon placates the uterosacral ligaments into the vaginal cuff, ensuring proper alignment and tension.
A bulge is visible in the vaginal area, created by the insertion of surgical devices.
The video shows the apple probe on the left, purchased by Cooper Surgical, and large steel blunt EA sizers on the right side.
The surgeon places a suture in the pubocervical fascia, which is clearly visible as the cuff is pushed upwards.
The surgeon carefully peels back the bladder to improve visibility of the underlying anatomical structures, revealing the pubocervical fascia.
The surgical field is exposed to allow for a clearer view of the second layer closure, with visible anatomical structures and instruments aiding in the procedure.
The surgeon covers the first layer of sutures with a second layer, demonstrating the improved coverage and reinforcement of the surgical site.
The surgeon cuts and removes sutures, revealing the advantages of the vessel ciliary extension and the dissection technique used in the double layer closure.
The surgical field shows intact vaginal support with no visible char on the tissue.
